title: Cat Cloudy Blue Pupils: Understanding Lenticular Sclerosis
meta_desc: Notice a bluish haze in your cat's eyes? Learn why this aging change is usually low urgency and how to distinguish it from cataracts.
content_html:
What is the bluish haze?

A cloudy, bluish-grey haze in an older cat's pupils is most commonly a condition called lenticular sclerosis, also known as nuclear sclerosis. This is a normal part of the aging process where the lens fibers become denser and more compressed over time. While it changes the appearance of the eye, it is generally transparent and allows light to pass through to the retina.

Is this an emergency?

The urgency level for this condition is low. Lenticular sclerosis is not painful and does not cause sudden blindness. However, it is important to distinguish it from cataracts, which are opaque and can lead to inflammation or vision loss. If your cat's eye appears suddenly white rather than blue, or if you notice redness, squinting, or rubbing at the eye, you should schedule a veterinary visit more promptly.

Why a photo helps triage

Capturing a clear, well-lit photo of your cat's eyes can significantly assist a veterinary professional in a remote assessment. A photo helps the vet see the depth and texture of the cloudiness. In lenticular sclerosis, the vet can often still see through to the back of the eye in a photo, whereas a cataract will look like a solid, milky obstruction. This visual data helps determine how quickly your cat needs to be seen in person.

faqs.question: Can my cat still see with this bluish haze?
faqs.answer: Yes, cats with lenticular sclerosis typically retain functional vision, though they may have slight difficulty with depth perception or focusing on very close objects.

faqs.question: Is lenticular sclerosis the same as a cataract?
faqs.answer: No. While both cause cloudiness, lenticular sclerosis is a normal aging change that stays somewhat transparent, whereas cataracts are a pathological change that becomes opaque and blocks vision.

faqs.question: At what age do cats usually develop this haze?
faqs.answer: Most cats begin to show visible signs of lenticular sclerosis around 9 to 12 years of age, though it can vary based on the individual.
